skill.name: memory-forensics
skill.description: Master memory forensics techniques including memory acquisition, process analysis, and artifact extraction using Volatility and related tools. Use when analyzing memory dumps, investigating incidents, or performing malware analysis.
skill.summary: Comprehensive guide to memory forensics acquisition, analysis, and artifact extraction using Volatility 3 and related tools for incident response.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a legitimate defensive security documentation skill for memory forensics. All 86 static findings are false positives: command examples are documentation for forensic tools (Volatility, WinPmem, LiME), Cobalt Strike references are YARA detection rules for defensive use, and privilege escalation patterns are standard forensic acquisition techniques. Safe for marketplace publication.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Commands Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: Command-line examples shown throughout the skill are documentation for forensic tools, not executable code. Examples include Volatility plugins, WinPmem, and LiME usage - all legitimate defensive security tools.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill is a documentation guide containing example commands for forensic tools. No actual code execution occurs within the skill itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cobalt Strike Detection Rule
dangerous_patterns.description: YARA rule example for detecting Cobalt Strike beacons in memory - this is defensive security content for malware detection, not malicious code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is a standard YARA rule for detecting Cobalt Strike beacons - legitimate defensive security technique used by security analysts.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privilege Escalation Patterns
dangerous_patterns.description: sudo commands shown are standard forensic acquisition techniques requiring root privileges (loading LiME kernel module, accessing /dev/mem).
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These are standard Linux memory acquisition commands that require elevated privileges - legitimate forensic workflow.

content.value_statement: Memory forensics is essential for detecting advanced threats that evade disk-based analysis. This skill provides comprehensive guidance on acquiring memory dumps and analyzing them with Volatility 3 to extract artifacts, detect malware, and investigate incidents.
actual_capabilities: Guide users through memory acquisition for Windows, Linux, and macOS systems using industry-standard tools
Provide Volatility 3 command syntax for process analysis, network connections, DLL enumeration, and registry parsing
Explain detection patterns for process injection, rootkits, and credential theft in memory
Offer YARA rule examples for scanning memory dumps for malware indicators
Document analysis workflows for malware analysis and incident response scenarios
Explain Windows kernel data structures (EPROCESS, PEB, VAD) relevant to memory analysis
limitations: Cannot actually acquire memory or run Volatility - provides guidance only
Does not include live malware samples or attack tools
Does not provide pre-built symbol files or YARA rules - users must obtain these separately
Cannot analyze memory dumps directly - requires Volatility installation and user action

use_cases.title: Incident Response Investigation
use_cases.description: Analyze a compromised system memory dump to identify malicious processes, network connections, and persistence mechanisms used by attackers.

use_cases.title: Malware Artifact Extraction
use_cases.description: Extract suspicious executables and payloads from process memory for further analysis in a sandbox environment.

use_cases.title: Enterprise Forensic Analysis
use_cases.description: Perform systematic memory forensics as part of a broader forensic investigation to establish timelines and identify attacker activity.

prompt_templates.title: Memory Acquisition Guide
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to acquire memory from a Windows 10 system for forensic analysis. What tools should I use and what are the recommended commands?
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to capture memory from a live Windows system

prompt_templates.title: Process Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a memory dump file. How do I use Volatility 3 to list all running processes and identify hidden or suspicious processes?
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to analyze processes in a memory dump

prompt_templates.title: Injection Detection
prompt_templates.prompt: What Volatility commands can help me detect code injection and DLL injection in a Windows memory dump?

prompt_templates.title: Credential Extraction
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I extract password hashes and LSA secrets from a Windows memory dump using Volatility?
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to recover credentials from memory

output_examples.input: How do I analyze a memory dump for network connections?
output_examples.output: Use Volatility 3 windows.netscan to list network connections. For a memory dump file named 'memory.raw', run: vol -f memory.raw windows.netscan. This shows active connections including local/remote addresses, ports, and state. Use windows.netstat for connection state information.

output_examples.input: What is the workflow for analyzing malware in memory?
output_examples.output: Follow this workflow: (1) Run windows.pstree and windows.pslist for process overview, (2) Use windows.netscan for network artifacts, (3) Run windows.malfind to detect code injection, (4) Analyze suspicious processes with windows.dlllist, (5) Dump suspicious executables with pslist --pid <PID> --dump, (6) Extract strings and run YARA scanning.

best_practices: Always acquire memory first before any disk analysis to preserve volatile data
Hash memory dumps immediately after acquisition to maintain chain of custody
Use multiple Volatility plugins to cross-reference findings and validate results
Document all commands, timestamps, and findings throughout the analysis
anti_patterns: Do not analyze memory dumps on the same system that generated them to avoid contamination
Do not rely on a single plugin result - always cross-reference with alternative plugins
Do not skip symbol table configuration - incorrect symbols lead to false negatives

faq.question: What tools do I need to start memory forensics?
faq.answer: You need a memory acquisition tool (WinPmem for Windows, LiME for Linux, osxpmem for macOS) and the Volatility 3 framework. Install with: pip install volatility3

faq.question: How do I choose the right Volatility profile?
faq.answer: The profile must match the operating system version and build number of the memory dump. Use windows.info (Volatility 2) or identify during analysis. Incorrect profiles cause parsing errors.

faq.question: Can I analyze Linux memory dumps with Volatility 3?
faq.answer: Yes, Volatility 3 supports Linux analysis with plugins like linux.pslist, linux.pstree, linux.bash, and linux.sockstat. Ensure you have the correct Linux profile.

faq.question: How do I detect hidden processes in memory?
faq.answer: Compare output from windows.pslist (active processes) with windows.psscan (scanned from memory pool). Hidden processes appear in psscan but not pslist. Use windows.pstree for parent-child relationships.

faq.question: What is the difference between windows.malfind and windows.ldrmodules?
faq.answer: malfind detects code injection by finding executable memory regions with suspicious protection flags. ldrmodules finds DLLs that are loaded but not visible in the PEB - a sign of DLL injection.

faq.question: How do I extract files from memory?
faq.answer: Use windows.dumpfiles to extract files from memory and windows.memmap --pid <PID> --dump to dump a specific process memory space. Note that encrypted files may not be recoverable.
